title: Rank the Stack
instructions: Each scenario gives you a single jurisdiction, one issue, and a stack of authorities in no particular order. Assign each authority to a tier by its binding effect. The tiers are ordered top to bottom; within a tier, order does not matter — ties are expected and correct. Check your work to see the reasoning and a model ranking.

tiers.label: Controls
tiers.blurb: Binding here and at the top of the hierarchy for this issue — this is what settles the question.

tiers.label: Binding, subordinate
tiers.blurb: Must be followed, but yields to anything in ‘Controls’ if they conflict.

tiers.label: Persuasive
tiers.blurb: Not binding here; the court may consider it.

tiers.label: Secondary / finding tool
tiers.blurb: Never the law itself — where you start, not where you end.

scenarios.title: Arizona landlord-tenant issue
scenarios.context: You are before an Arizona trial court (Superior Court) on a question of Arizona landlord-tenant law. Rank these authorities by binding effect on that court.

authorities.label: An on-point Arizona statute (Arizona Revised Statutes) setting the landlord’s duty
authorities.why: An on-point statute from the forum’s own jurisdiction is binding and sits at the top of the enacted-law hierarchy for this issue. It controls — tied with the state high court’s reading of it.

authorities.label: An Arizona Supreme Court decision interpreting that statute
authorities.why: The state’s highest court, binding on the trial court, and authoritative on what the statute means. It works together with the statute — you cannot rank one beneath the other, so both control.

authorities.label: An Arizona Court of Appeals decision applying the statute
authorities.why: Binding on the trial court (a higher court in the same system), but subordinate to both the statute and the Arizona Supreme Court above it.

authorities.label: An Arizona administrative regulation on a related detail
authorities.why: A validly issued regulation binds, but a statute outranks a regulation on the same subject — so it sits below the controlling statute.

authorities.label: A California Supreme Court decision on a similar statute
authorities.why: Another jurisdiction’s law does not bind an Arizona court. Useful if Arizona law were unsettled, but persuasive only.

authorities.label: A law review article analyzing the issue
authorities.why: A secondary source: it can orient you and point to primary authority, but it is never the law and never binds.

authorities.label: A legal encyclopedia entry on landlord duties
authorities.why: Secondary authority — a starting place for orientation, not something a court must (or may) treat as law.

scenarios.title: Federal statutory question
scenarios.context: You are before a U.S. District Court sitting in the Ninth Circuit, on a question under a federal statute. Rank these authorities by binding effect on that court.

authorities.label: The on-point federal statute (U.S. Code)
authorities.why: The governing enacted law itself, binding nationwide within federal authority. It controls — tied with the Supreme Court’s interpretation of it.

authorities.label: A U.S. Supreme Court decision interpreting that statute
authorities.why: The top of the federal judicial system; binding on every federal court and authoritative on the statute’s meaning. Statute and Supreme Court reading control together.

authorities.label: A Ninth Circuit decision interpreting the statute
authorities.why: Binding on the district court (its own circuit), but subordinate to the Supreme Court and the statute above it.

authorities.label: A federal regulation implementing the statute
authorities.why: A valid regulation binds, but it draws its force from the statute and yields to it — so it ranks below the controlling statute.

authorities.label: A Second Circuit decision on the same statute
authorities.why: A sister circuit does not bind a district court in the Ninth Circuit. Persuasive — the court may weigh its reasoning but need not follow it.

authorities.label: A treatise on the statutory scheme
authorities.why: Secondary authority: helpful for understanding the framework and finding cases, but never binding.
